text: Hey, everybody.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Welcome back to today's podcast.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And on today's show, we're going to talk about something a little bit different than a coding practice or a design pattern or anything of that nature.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And the topic is adhering to the existing code standards of a code base that you enter.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, that may sound kind of obscure, but for the veterans who listen to the show, anyone who's been around for a little while, any developers who've joined multiple different teams and seen many different code bases,
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: you notice that not every code base is the same in fact some code bases will follow a particular style guide and it's very structured and they'll use plugins into their ide's that say the code has to look a certain way maybe there's a space after
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: the you know any of the operators like plus or minus maybe the new the bracket that's on the same line as an if statement has to go on the next line like c sharp maybe there's certain ways that a team does things and i've seen this countless times throughout my career now when i'm writing my own code i have my own style guide that i follow and usually what i follow is going to be what's kind of the industry norm that's put out by
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And accepted by the wide, vast group consensus of developers out there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And if this is going to be in Android, it might be a stock guide that's put out by a popular company that a lot of people adopt.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It might be something by Google.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If it's JavaScript, it might be some popular JavaScript format using ESLint to verify that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If it's Ruby, it might be standard RB.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's a number of ways that you can do this.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The topic in which I'm talking about today is when you actually show up at a client or at a new job and you look at the code base and you see some code that's just really funky.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The way they format it is really weird.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And to give you an example, one time I was working over at Medtronic, which is a medical tech company.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and this is probably close to 10 to 12 years ago.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: We had written some code for them at this consulting agency that I was at, and every new function had to start with a three-line comment, and the top line had to be blank, the bottom line had to be blank, but the two slashes for the comment, this was a C-sharp language, the two slashes at the top and bottom had to be empty, so there was nothing there, and your comment could only go in the middle.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And this was to document every function.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This was not a standard norm by any stretch of the imagination.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It was just how that was done there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, to me, this looked really funky.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It was a waste of space.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I already knew that most of these code comments would not get updated.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And furthermore, it just looked really cluttered to me.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: However, what I learned over the course of many years prior to that is there's two ways to go about this.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I can either hop into the code and do it my way, which I think is correct.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And maybe I'm adhering to industry standards and therefore I must be correct and arrogant in that regard.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Or I accept and adopt.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The code base is standards.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So which one should you do?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Well, it kind of really depends on a couple of situations.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: A situation one is you're working for a company or maybe you're the new person there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And in that situation, you join a company and they have a set of standards that they follow.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: In that case, I recommend that you follow their set of standards.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If they have a certain way of documenting their code, then you should adopt that as well.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: if they don't document anything at all we'll get to that in a second but let's say they have some type of documentation or they have some standard they follow you should stick to that now why is that why should you adhere to a particular standard when it's something that you're not familiar with well mainly because it's going to be a way that you can
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: ensure that your code is going to be readable across your team and your team members when they pull your code down are going to be able to read your code and see what it does it's going to make sense to them see there's something that happens in your head when you read code and all of a sudden when you go to another file and the format is vastly different
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: what you're going to notice is there's like this kind of jarring sensation that happens inside of you because the pattern recognition that you're used to seeing is not there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Boom, this code has a different format.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Like, wow, I don't even know what I'm looking at.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: in fact you've probably had this happen when you go look at into some source code for an open source project you hop into it and you're completely lost and it does not make any sense whatsoever of what's happening or why it's doing what it's doing because of just the different coding standards they've done so this is why you should follow the existing coding standard that has been set in place for a particular company or wherever you're working at you can this could be full-time consulting freelancing whatever
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: that kind of just creates a cohesiveness around the actual project and allows you to view and read the code accordingly.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: All right.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So let's take the flip side of that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: We have the other option here is you've went to a company.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This is kind of, you know, one A and you went to a company and they don't have a standard.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: What do you do in that situation?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: In that situation, what you're going to do is you're going to have maybe the opportunity to implement some type of standard.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: What I recommend here is finding a common standard that's available out there, that's documented and supported by the community.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then at that point, you can adopt it and then educate the team members on this is why it's good to adopt a common style amongst your code.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, again, there's a bunch of them out there that you could use.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I'm not going to tell you which one that you should use.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I just tend to follow whichever one is the most popular and widely accepted in the given industry that I'm working in.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if it's JavaScript, it might be JavaScript.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If it's Kotlin, it'll be a Kotlin one, Java, whatever.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You kind of get the point.
speaker: SPEAKER_00

text: This will allow you to also become an educator within your team.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And your team is going to see you as a leader in the team because you're trying to improve the code quality.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're going to try to improve the communication, trying to improve everything that's going on between the team because the code is the source of truth of everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If there's a bug, what do we have to do?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: We have to go into the code base and figure out what's going on.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And the more times we have to do a context switch because developer A made the code look a certain way and developer B made the code look a certain way and developer C made the code look a certain way.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Well, then it gets really, really difficult.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's hard to really understand what's going on.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It really mentally taxes you at the end of the day.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that's something that you would really want to try to avoid.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So if you can introduce some type of common coding style guide and structure and so forth, you're going to be an unsung hero to the team and also help lead the team to much more code cohesion, cleanliness, and code quality at the end of the day.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, the other option is that you just don't adopt any coding practice whatsoever and you just kind of cowboy code it up and you just kind of get the stuff done and ship it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Is there anything wrong with that?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: some craftsman of the software craftsman would say that there was a problem with that but at the end of the day the computer really doesn't care what your code looks like as long as it compiles and runs it'll work but that's not too considerate of your future self or any future developers that you're working with so I wouldn't really highly advise you go down kind of option B of just saying you know what I don't care I'm just going to write this code and get it done whatever
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: I highly recommend that you follow the existing style guide in there.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, there's kind of a final option here of what do you do when you've maybe entered a company?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And this could be considered option three.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You've entered a company.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's been an existing style guide.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It didn't really work out that well.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: They realize it's not that great.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Perhaps there's a bunch of different file formats lying around or coding styles and formats lying around and you're wondering what you should be doing.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: At that point in time, you can do a couple of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: One, you could take a look at the existing style that's inside of the code base and see if one of them actually is quite standardized.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If it is, you might want to just bolt onto that one because it's going to be easier to maintain that standard and slowly adapt it to maybe a more widely accepted format.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Another option is going to be just to completely start afresh and say, all right, we're going to start using this new standard of coding.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And here's the new style.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then what you start doing from that point forward is following that new style.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Then as you touch any existing code that you have interacted with, maybe you need to go look at an old class file, update something that's happening, change that feature.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: As soon as you touch it, you update that file to match the new format of the coding standard.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This, in my opinion, is going to be the best approach if you have a large legacy application that has a lot of code.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And you just don't want to go back through and just adjust the code just for the sake of making the style look better.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you're inside of the class, you're making a change.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: That is the premier opportunity for you to improve the code quality and make it match the existing style format.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So that's what I'm going to recommend in that approach is that you find something that your team agrees on and stick with it.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you've maybe adopted a project, you're the only developer.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Find again, find that standard you want to work with and just stick with that.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then don't let the overwhelming 20,000 files that are sitting on your drive that are not in the new format bother you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Just know, hey, going forward, all the code is going to be in this new format.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And anytime I touch any one of these old files, I'm going to update that code to match the new coding standard.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, there's a bunch of tools out there that are going to allow you to help you to do this.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And as I mentioned, it depends on the framework and the platform in which you're working on.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you're working on something like Kotlin or the JVM, you want to go ahead and look at any of the different style.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: plugins that you can use that will allow you to specify that, hey, this file is not following a style guide.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: These things will plug into Android Studio, IntelliJ, and so forth, and will show you the errors.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Furthermore, they can plug into your build system and let you know that, hey, this file is not adhering to the standard that we have been set.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Lastly,
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: in that same regard is if you have a number of files that are not going to match that again maybe you've got those 20 000 files lying around and you're not going to be able to get to them until you touch them or update that feature so maybe 90 of those you're never going to change that's fine
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So what you can do inside of the build process and the plugins at that point is you can tell the build process, look, go ahead and ignore all of these files or this file glob pattern, and don't worry about these.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And it won't process those.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then any new files that come along, it'll automatically include those ones inside of a build process.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: The same thing will happen in not just Android and Kotlin Java.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: But same thing in applications like Ruby.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's standard RB.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's plugins for Visual Studio Code, RubyMine.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If you're using Python, you'll find plugins for PyCharm.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Again, VS Code, if you're using that for Python.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And there's all different types of things.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So once you find that standard that you want to stick to, you can go ahead and look up coding styles.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: and so forth, coding standards for your given language and see if you can find a plugin for it that'll work with your IDE as well as your build system and then figure out how to hook it into your build system.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And that's really the last step here is automating this whole process because you wanna catch these things as soon as it happened.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And as soon as you catch these issues, the cheaper it gets.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And I read this somewhere from Martin Fowler and I forget what blog post it is.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And if I can find it, I'll shove it into the show notes.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It was in an article he wrote many years ago, and he outlined of how when you'll find a bug in development, it is exponentially cheaper when you fix it in development than fixing it in production.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Now, I'm not saying that your coding style is going to perhaps introduce bugs, though that could happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: What I'm trying to say is the earlier you find any issue inside of your code base, the cheaper and easier it is going to be for you to fix.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: So as much of this that you can automate through plugins that will just run in your IDE as you're typing your code to CICD pipelines that will fail the build if you don't pass a certain style guide, the better.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And the reason simply is because later down the road, again, as you open your code, you're going to start seeing a pattern.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And when your brain operates on a certain way, it's very pattern oriented.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And you'll notice this if you've looked a lot of logs from servers, maybe you're an Android developer, you're used to looking at Logcat or Ruby, you're used to looking at the Ruby server output.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You'll see certain patterns that happen.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: An exception occurs.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You'll see the format of the log almost changes.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And you know exactly what you're looking through.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You could scroll through 10,000 lines of log code.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And you can almost see exactly where an exception is.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You may not know what it is, but you'll know, hey, that block, something happened.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You can zoom in on that and see, oh, here's an exception happened.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: How did that happen?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's because you're doing pattern recognition.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Same thing goes when you're working with code.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: If your code has the same format all over the place, you're going to find it much easier to work with.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You're going to find it much easier to maintain.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: It's going to have less cognitive load on you.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And frankly, it's just going to actually...
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: wear you out less.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Because if you have code in 10 different formats and 10 different files and one file puts the variables at the top and the next file puts the variables at the bottom and the next line puts the variables wherever they're declared or the constants or whatever you want to call them, it's just going to be really confusing because you're going to open that file and be like, okay, where's the constants?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: well, they're supposed to be at the top.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: And then you're like, well, why are they at the bottom?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Like, okay, I need to remove these to the top.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: This is dumb.

text: And these are the things that go through your head.

text: But then after you say that, you move these things around and now you have to slow down again and change gears.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You have to change context again.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Like, okay, what was I doing again?

text: Was I fixing a bug?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: Was I implementing that feature?
speaker: SPEAKER_01

speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: You know where to find everything.
speaker: SPEAKER_01

text: There's less cognitive load.
